subject: Careers
topic: Career 8 Mosaic - Week 4 Technology and Identity

secondary_architecture_support.note: The inquiry can be compressed into a shorter sequence, but the staged packet, Day 3 checkpoint, and Day 4 final synthesis still need the full flow.

materials_control_note: The weekly packet holds film follow-along notes, risk/affected/respond organizer work, research notes, and final product planning only. The Day 3 checkpoint sheet stays teacher-facing. The single primary evidence artifact for the week stays in the Day 4 final response sheet.
grade_subject_fit: Fits BC Career Education 8 by centering digital responsibility, public identity, technology risks, who is affected, and realistic career connections to fields that help respond in BC contexts.
unit_context: Week 4 of a six-week Career 8 Mosaic unit. This week builds on Week 1 self-knowledge, Week 2 decision-making, and Week 3 contribution by asking how technology shapes learning, work, identity, and responsibility, and by highlighting careers and fields that help respond when digital systems create problems.
assignment_purpose: Students investigate digital risks through The Social Dilemma and related inquiry tasks, compare possible response roles, and then create a Digital Risk, Real Careers product that connects one digital risk to one realistic career or field, explains how that role helps respond, interprets why the match is realistic, includes one BC connection, and reflects on possible personal fit.
final_evidence_target: A Day 4 Digital Risk, Real Careers product in the final response sheet that names one clear digital risk, identifies one realistic responding career or field, explains how the role responds, interprets why the role is a strong match, includes one BC connection, and includes a personal-fit reflection.

student_task_flow.step: Observe digital risks, effects, and response questions during the film
student_task_flow.label: Day 1
student_task_flow.evidence: Students use a follow-along sheet to notice risks, who is affected, and who might help respond.

student_task_flow.step: Build a risk / affected / respond bridge
student_task_flow.label: Day 2
student_task_flow.evidence: Students move from naming a problem to explaining who is affected and which roles or fields help respond.

student_task_flow.label: Day 3
student_task_flow.evidence: Students compare possible fields, reject weak pairings, gather BC-relevant notes, and conference for release.

student_task_flow.label: Day 4
student_task_flow.evidence: Students complete the Digital Risk, Real Careers final response in the Day 4 final response sheet only.

success_criteria: I can explain one clear digital risk without using vague fear language.
I can identify who is affected by that risk.
I can name one realistic career or field that helps respond and explain what that role does.
I can include one BC-relevant connection that makes the issue or role feel real here.
I can complete a clear Day 4 final product on the final response sheet.

supports_scaffolds.label: Movie follow-along document
supports_scaffolds.support: Students record risks, affected groups, and response questions while watching instead of reacting only from memory.

supports_scaffolds.label: Risk / Who is affected / Who helps respond organizer
supports_scaffolds.support: Students practice the full logic chain instead of stopping at the problem statement.

supports_scaffolds.label: Risk-to-career matching bank
supports_scaffolds.support: The packet and class modelling steer students toward realistic response roles instead of random pairings.

supports_scaffolds.label: Teacher model of one full chain
supports_scaffolds.support: Students see a concrete example that includes the risk, who is affected, the responding field, a BC connection, and a short fit reflection.

supports_scaffolds.label: Research notes organizer
supports_scaffolds.support: Students gather targeted notes on one risk and one field before final writing.

supports_scaffolds.label: Sentence frames
supports_scaffolds.support: Frames help students explain how a role responds and why the match is realistic.

supports_scaffolds.label: Product checklist
supports_scaffolds.support: Students can verify that their final response includes all required parts before handing it in.

supports_scaffolds.label: Choice in final structure
supports_scaffolds.support: Students may present the final response in labeled bullet clusters or short structured explanation blocks suited to Chromebook use.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use the follow-along sheet, organizer, matching bank, sentence frames, and teacher check-ins to help students move from broad reactions to one specific risk-response chain.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Move through the weekly packet, test one realistic digital risk and response field, and complete a clear final product with a BC connection and fit reflection.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Push students to compare more than one affected group, explain tradeoffs in a response system, or distinguish between a primary responding role and a supporting role.

checkpoint_release_logic: Keep the weekly packet as planning-only evidence until Day 3.

Release the Day 4 final response sheet only after the teacher checkpoint confirms the student has one clear digital risk, one realistic career or field match, one valid explanation of who is affected and how the role responds, and one concrete BC connection.
teacher_implementation_notes: Balanced, inquiry-based frame. Technology is not the villain; risk, effect, response, and responsibility are the focus.
The key question keeps returning: who is affected, and who helps respond?
Packet should read as one staged four-day workflow, not disconnected media-literacy activities.
Career link stays realistic. Weak or random risk-to-career matches need redirection before final product release.
BC-facing examples help the response roles feel local and real.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think the point of the week is simply that phones or social media are bad.
Students may think only technology workers respond to digital problems.
Students may think any job can be connected to any digital risk if they explain it creatively enough.
Students may think public identity only matters for celebrities or adults and not for students, schools, or future workplaces.
Students may treat the BC connection as an extra detail instead of part of the actual response chain.
assessment_focus: Clarity of the student's digital risk statement
Accuracy and realism of the student's risk-to-career pairing
Strength of the explanation of who is affected and how the role responds
Specificity of the BC connection and personal-fit reflection

lesson_overview.overview: Week 4 of the six-week Career 8 Mosaic unit focuses on digital life, public identity, technology risks, and the roles people play when digital systems create problems. Across four days, students use The Social Dilemma and related inquiry tasks to notice risks, identify affected groups, test realistic responding fields, and build one BC-relevant career connection. The week ends with a Digital Risk, Real Careers product that explains one digital risk, one realistic career or field, how that role responds, one BC connection, and whether that role might fit the student.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How does technology shape learning, work, and public identity, and what kinds of careers help solve the problems technology creates?

sequence.focus: Film framing, observation, and first noticing.
sequence.carryover: Students begin noticing digital risks while tracking who is affected and who might help respond.

sequence.focus: Risk / affected / respond bridge activity and project launch.
sequence.carryover: Students move from naming a problem to building a realistic risk-response chain.

sequence.focus: Guided research and realistic risk-to-career matching.
sequence.carryover: Students prepare one specific, plausible risk-to-career product idea before final writing.

sequence.focus: Final synthesis, selected sharing, and reflection.
sequence.carryover: The final evidence remains in the Day 4 final response sheet only.

integrity_checks: The weekly packet remains one coherent staged workflow instead of disconnected film notes and project pages.
The Day 3 checkpoint sheet stays teacher-facing and supports release logic rather than student practice.
The Day 4 final response sheet is the single primary evidence artifact for the week.
The technology lens stays balanced and inquiry-based rather than anti-technology.
The career connection stays explicit, realistic, and BC-relevant.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Technology creates opportunities and risks. In Week 4, students examine how digital tools shape learning, work, and public identity, while also learning that different roles and fields help respond when digital systems create problems.
learning_goals: I can frame the film and discussion work as inquiry rather than anti-technology messaging.
I can help students move from naming a digital risk to identifying who is affected and who helps respond.
I can guard against weak or random risk-to-career matches by insisting on realistic response roles.
I can use the Day 3 checkpoint to confirm whether students have enough concrete material to build a strong Day 4 final product.
materials: One weekly Career Mosaic packet for Week 4
The Social Dilemma or approved teacher-selected excerpts
Optional BC-facing research links or local examples
Chromebooks or classroom devices for Day 3 research and Day 4 writing

timing.activity: Frame the week with the sentence: Technology is not the villain here. The question is: when digital tools create problems, who notices, who gets affected, and who helps fix them?
Watch The Social Dilemma or approved clips while students complete the follow-along organizer in the packet.
Begin a short debrief focused on risks, affected groups, and early questions about who helps respond.
Re-open the film ideas and launch the risk / affected / respond bridge activity.
Use sorting, partner talk, or class examples to test which careers or fields realistically respond to different digital risks.
Students complete the organizer and choose one risk-response direction for deeper research.
Model one full chain from digital risk to affected group to responding field to BC connection.
Students use the matching bank and guided notes organizer to research one realistic pairing.
Teacher conferences with the checkpoint sheet to confirm clear risk, realistic field match, affected group, response logic, and BC connection.
Re-open the final product checklist and model the tone of a concise, evidence-based Digital Risk, Real Careers response.
Students complete the final product using packet notes and structured response labels.
Students share selected examples and reflect on one risk and the roles that help respond to it.

teacher_notes: Do not let the week collapse into generic anti-phone or anti-social-media messaging.
Keep asking what makes a response role realistic. A strong match is a role that would actually do something about the selected risk.
Use BC-facing examples such as schools, public services, local media, community mental-health supports, legal or policy settings, or technology workplaces students could realistically encounter here.
Treat the packet as a build. The Day 4 final response should feel earned by the earlier organizer and research work.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think the film proves technology itself is the main problem instead of showing how systems and design choices create risks.
Students may think the people affected are only the users and miss families, schools, workplaces, or communities.
Students may think a responding role only counts if it is a coder or app designer.
Students may choose flashy jobs with no real connection to the selected risk.
likely_hesitations: Some students may have strong opinions about phones or social media but struggle to move into evidence-based explanation.
Some students may avoid choosing a single risk because several feel relevant.
Some students may find the BC connection harder than the risk itself unless given local prompts.
teacher_moves: Use the frame: The point is not just to name a problem. The point is to understand who is affected and who helps respond.
Ask: What would this person or field actually do in response to the risk?
Ask: If that role disappeared, who would still have to deal with the problem?
Ask: What makes this connection real in BC and not just technically possible somewhere?
model_reflection_language: One digital risk I notice is misinformation because people can make decisions using false or manipulated content.
People affected might include students, families, voters, or community members who depend on trustworthy information.
One realistic responding field is journalism because journalists verify sources, investigate claims, and help the public sort fact from manipulation.
A BC connection could be local news, public-information work, or schools and communities needing accurate information here.
This role might fit me if I like research, asking careful questions, and helping people make better decisions.
look_fors: Student can state one digital risk clearly and specifically.
Student can explain who is affected by that risk.
Student can name one realistic responding field and explain what it actually does.
Student can include one BC connection that feels concrete and relevant.
Student is building enough material to complete a clear Day 4 final product.
support_moves: Ask: Who is affected besides the obvious first group?
Ask: What kind of work would actually help respond here?
Ask: Is this a strong pairing, or just a job name placed next to the risk?
Offer sentence frames and allow labeled bullet planning before final writing.
future_pathway_connection: If students are ready, extend the inquiry by comparing more than one responding field connected to the same risk.
Keep the extension optional. The core outcome for this week is one clear, realistic risk-to-response chain.

task_sheet.title: Week 4 Career Mosaic Packet - Technology and Identity
task_sheet.purpose_line: Collect notes across the week, then build one clear Digital Risk, Real Careers product.
instructions: Work through the packet across the week. Each part helps with the next one.
Talk first when useful, then keep your own notes.

tasks.label: Day 1 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Before the film, record one opportunity you see in digital tools and one question you want to keep in mind while watching.

render_hints.heading: Day 1 - Pre-film frame
render_hints.help: The goal is not to start with technology as the villain. The goal is to notice risk, effect, and response more clearly.
record_fields: A digital tool or platform I use for learning, life, or entertainment
One opportunity it creates
One risk or question I want to watch for
The question I will keep asking: who is affected, and who helps respond?

tasks.label: Day 1 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Use this follow-along organizer during or after the film to capture one strong risk-response chain.

render_hints.heading: Day 1 - Film follow-along
render_hints.help: Use short notes. You are collecting a strong example, not summarizing the whole film.
record_fields: One digital risk I noticed in the film
Who seemed affected by it
One example or scene that made the risk feel real
Who might help respond to that risk
One question I still have

tasks.label: Day 1 / Part C
tasks.prompt: Complete the debrief frames using what you noticed from the film and class discussion.

render_hints.heading: Day 1 - First noticing
render_hints.help: Keep the balanced frame. The point is not just to name a problem. The point is to understand who is affected and who helps respond.
blank_prompts: Technology is not the villain here because ___.
One digital risk that felt real to me was ___.
People affected by that risk could include ___.
A role or field that might help respond is ___.

tasks.label: Day 2 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Use one risk from class to build the full bridge from problem to affected group to response role.

render_hints.heading: Day 2 - Risk / Affected / Respond bridge
render_hints.help: Do not stop at the risk. Keep the whole logic visible.
record_fields: The digital risk I am focusing on
Who is affected by it
How the risk can affect learning, work, identity, or community life
Who helps respond
Why that response role matters

tasks.label: Day 2 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Match each digital risk with the responding career or field that makes the most sense. Star the pair you want to keep for your project.

render_hints.heading: Day 2 - Risk-to-career matching bank
render_hints.help: After you match, star one pair you want to keep. If you change the risk later, use the same logic: choose a role that would actually respond to the problem.

right_items: Software testing, data ethics, or policy
Digital forensics, law, or investigative journalism

tasks.label: Day 2 / Part C
tasks.prompt: Keep one strong pair from the bank and explain why it fits. Then name one weak or random match you ruled out.

render_hints.heading: Day 2 - Match check
render_hints.help: This is the part where you prove the match is realistic, not random.
record_fields: The risk I want to keep
The strongest responding career or field and why it fits
One weak or random match I ruled out and why

tasks.label: Day 3 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Use guided research notes to build one strong digital risk and response chain.

render_hints.heading: Day 3 - Research notes organizer
render_hints.help: Keep your notes targeted to the final product. You do not need a full report.
record_fields: My chosen digital risk
My chosen career or field
What the role actually does to help respond
Who is affected by the risk
One source detail, example, or real-world note that strengthens the explanation

tasks.label: Day 3 / Part B
tasks.prompt: Add the BC connection and a short personal-fit note before the checkpoint.

render_hints.heading: Day 3 - BC connection and fit
render_hints.help: The BC connection should feel real here: local media, schools, public services, community supports, technology workplaces, or public-facing systems in the province.
record_fields: One BC connection that makes this risk or role feel real here
Why the BC connection matters
One reason this role might fit me
One reason I still need to think more about the fit

tasks.label: Day 3 / Part C
tasks.prompt: Complete the final product planner so you are ready for the checkpoint and Day 4 final writing.

render_hints.heading: Day 3 - Ready for Day 4
render_hints.help: Plan here. Move your final version to Day 4.
blank_prompts: The digital risk I will explain is ___.
The career or field I will connect to it is ___.
This role might fit me because ___.

tasks.label: Day 4 / Part A
tasks.prompt: Use this checklist to make sure your final product has each part before you move it to the Day 4 sheet.

render_hints.heading: Day 4 - Product checklist and transfer plan
render_hints.help: Choose the clearest structure: labeled bullets or short explanation blocks.
record_fields: My structure choice: labeled bullets or short explanation blocks
My risk statement is ready
My career or field match is realistic
My BC connection is concrete
My personal-fit reflection is specific

days.carryover_note: Students begin noticing digital risks, affected groups, and response questions while watching the film and starting the debrief.

content.subtitle: Technology and Identity: what digital tools make possible, and what happens when they create problems.

content.scenario: Technology is not the villain. When digital tools create problems, who is affected and who helps respond?
prompts: What opportunity do digital tools create?

rows: What digital risk is being described?
Who is affected by that risk?
Who should help respond, and what might they actually do?

content.task: Use your notes to name one risk, one affected group, and one response role.

prompts: Why is naming a problem not enough?
What question will you carry into tomorrow?

days.carryover_note: Students move from general film reactions into clearer risk / affected / respond chains and launch the project with stronger career matching.

content.subtitle: Risk, who is affected, and who helps respond.

content.scenario: Choose one digital risk from yesterday and trace who it affects and who helps respond.
prompts: Who could respond in a realistic way?

slides.title: The point is not just to name a problem

rows: A strong response starts with a clear digital risk.
It also explains who is affected and why the impact matters.
Then it identifies a realistic field or role that actually helps respond.

slides.title: Teacher model of one full chain

content.model: Risk: misinformation. Affected group: students, families, and community members. Field: journalism. Response: journalists verify sources. BC link: local news still matters. Fit: good for someone who likes research and careful questions.
supports: Keep the chain realistic and clear.

prompts: Which risk can you explain clearly?

days.carryover_note: Students research one realistic risk-to-career chain and conference before the final Day 4 product is released.

content.scenario: A strong match is a role that would actually respond to the problem.
prompts: What would the role really do?
Who is still affected if it disappears?
Does it make sense in BC?

items: The digital risk is clear and specific.
The affected group is named clearly.
The career or field would realistically help respond.
The BC connection feels concrete, not decorative.

content.task: Use the packet and matching bank to build one strong chain before the checkpoint.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Day 3 Checkpoint Sheet
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Teacher checkpoint before Day 4 Digital Risk, Real Careers release.
look_fors: Student can state one clear digital risk.
Student can identify who is affected by the risk.
Student can name one realistic career or field that helps respond.
Student can explain how the role responds and provide one BC connection.
Student is ready to move from packet planning to a cleaner Day 4 final product.
conference_prompts: What digital risk are you focusing on, and why is it specific enough?
Who is affected by this risk?
What would your chosen role or field actually do in response?
Why is this a strong match instead of a random pairing?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Release the Day 4 final response sheet only when the student can explain one clear digital risk, one realistic responding field, how the role responds, and one concrete BC connection.

days.carryover_note: Final evidence stays in the Day 4 Digital Risk, Real Careers product only.

content.subtitle: Digital Risk, Real Careers: show the risk, the effect, the response, and the BC connection.

slides.title: Model the structure, not the exact answer

content.model: Risk: digital footprint and reputation. Affected group: students and job applicants. Field: education and counselling. Response: teachers and counsellors help students protect future options. BC link: this matters in BC schools. Fit: good for someone who likes helping people think ahead.

content.goal: Turn your packet notes into one clear final product.
prompts: Use labeled bullets or short explanation blocks.
Show why the role matters, not just its name.
content.instruction: Move your final version onto the Day 4 sheet.

prompts: What digital risk do you understand better now?
What role or field do you respect more now?

final_response_sheet.title: Day 4 Digital Risk, Real Careers
final_response_sheet.prompt: Show one digital risk, who is affected, the career or field that helps respond, one BC connection, and whether the role might fit you.
planning_reminders: Choose one risk and one response field you can explain clearly.
Make sure the role would realistically respond to the selected risk.

render_hints.purpose_line: Keep the risk, response role, BC connection, and fit in separate parts.
render_hints.response_note: Keep each section specific and believable.
structured_labels: Career or field and how it responds
quick_check_items: I explained one clear digital risk.
My career or field match is realistic.
I showed how the role helps respond.
My fit reflection sounds honest and specific.

success_criteria: My final product explains one clear digital risk.
I identify one realistic career or field connected to that risk.
I explain how that role helps respond.
I include one BC connection and one personal-fit reflection.
My fit reflection sounds honest and specific.
